title: Introduce RAG cold-start warmup quickstart with edge prefetch and wasm preprocessor
preview: Motivation: cold-start latency is a common pain for edge-deployed RAG microservices where first requests trigger model warming and index priming; this quickstart demonstrates an opinionated pattern (edge prefetch + lightweight WASM request preprocessor + adaptive prefix cache) to reduce first-request tail latency without changing server-side runtime. What this PR adds: a new quickstart 'rag-coldstart-warmup' with README, a small TypeScript edge worker example that prefetches likely context, a WASM preprocessor Dockerfile and build scaffold, sample Terraform module to deploy to an edge Lambda/edge VM, a small indexed sample corpus and manifest.yaml, and a GH Actions smoke job that validates end-to-end cold-start latency under controlled conditions. Checklist: [x] Quickstart README with architecture diagram and step-by-step run instructions; [x] Local dev script (scripts/run-local.sh) that runs the quickstart in Docker with sample index; [x] Example prefetch policy (heuristics.json) and adaptive prefix cache config; [x] WASM preprocessor build + simple JS shim demonstrating header-based routing and request normalization; [x] Smoke CI job to verify startup and a simple query baseline. Usage summary: follow README to run locally: cd quickstarts/rag-coldstart-warmup && yarn && make build && ./scripts/run-local.sh --index small; the quickstart launches a light HTTP shim (edge prefetcher), a local mini-indexer, and a Redwood dev-runner configured to use a pinned small model variant; sample queries and expected latency numbers are in README. Implementation notes: the edge worker demonstrates speculative fetch of up to three candidate passages using low-cost metadata heuristics before the first token is requested; the WASM preprocessor normalizes incoming tool-call payloads and enforces a size cap to avoid oversized first requests; the adaptive prefix cache is configured to hold the top-K prefix KV entries per session for 30s by default which reduces warm KV misses for repeated short-lived sessions. CI/QA: GH Actions smoke job 'rag-warmup-smoke' executes a cold-start timeline measurement and fails if 95th percentile first-token latency exceeds 1.2x baseline; unit tests cover the preprocessor and heuristic policy; integration test runs a headless scenario with synthetic traffic. Backwards compatibility: purely opt-in quickstart; server side expects no protocol changes. Related items: reference design doc in DOCS-198 and ENG-3456 for runtime knobs and perf targets; follows patterns in existing quickstarts but emphasizes an edge-first prefetch strategy and a WASM-based preprocessing shim. Migration notes: none for runtime, this is training material and sample configs only. Merge summary and outcome: 6 commits squashed into one merge, all CI green after a single re-run of flaky e2e, merged via squash. Links: internal linear ENG-3456 (https://linear.app/company/ENG-3456) and DOCS-198 (https://linear.app/company/DOCS-198).

title: introduce-unified-rag-starterkit-multilang-bindings-and-docsite-snippets
preview:
Motivation: Customers repeatedly asked for a compact, multi-language RAG starter that is small enough to fork as a template but comprehensive enough to cover model selection, embeddings, caching, and observability. This PR introduces a unified starter kit that: 1) provides TypeScript, Python, and Go reference runners; 2) includes small telemetry adapter shims that wire into Redwood Console probes; 3) adds docsite snippets and quickstart exercises so users can get from zero to a running RAG demo in under 10 minutes.

What changed (high level): - new starter directory with language-specific runners and minimal infra (docker-compose + local index) - telemetry adapters that emit the same semantic events we use in the Console (session_start, retrieval_hit, model_call, latency_bucket) - compact README and step-by-step Quickstart docs added to the docs snippets directory - small helper library for typed adapter bindings (TS/Python/Go) to demonstrate cross-language contract patterns - CI smoke tests and a local e2e script to exercise streaming and embeddings.

Checklist: - [x] Add TS/Python/Go runner templates - [x] Implement telemetry shim and examples - [x] Add docsite snippet pages and quickstart guide - [x] Add CI smoke scenarios that run locally in GitHub Actions - [x] Add CHANGELOG entry and release notes draft - [x] Validate minimal resource configuration for local dev (docker-compose)

Testing notes: The branch contains a lightweight GH Action that runs the smoke scenario (index build + single query per runner). Initially the Python runner failed intermittently due to a race in the local index seeder; added a short retry loop and re-ran CI. All checks are passing at merge. Manual verification steps are listed in the Quickstart README and were validated on macOS and Linux in a dev VM.

Backward compatibility: No API surface changes to Redwood platforms. Everything here is sample code and docs; no production runtime changes. The typed adapter helpers are opt-in and live in the examples namespace.

Related: ENG-2142 (starter kit initiative), PM-1098 (quickstart conversion for docs site), SUP-4021 (customer ask from onboarding).

title: introduce-modular-rag-studio-lite-quickstart
preview: Adds a compact, modular RAG Studio quickstart aimed at developer demos and offline authoring. Motivation: we frequently field requests for a lightweight, dependency-minimal sandbox that demonstrates tool-calling, incremental indexing, and scenario-driven evaluations without needing heavy infra. This quickstart targets two personas: 1) engineers who want a reproducible local demo for product meetings and 2) contributors who need an isolated harness for writing scenario-based evals. Change summary: 1) new CLI scaffolder (bin/mini-rag) that bootstraps a tiny web UI, an in-memory incremental indexer, and a WASM-based tool stub runtime; 2) compact devcontainer and Docker profile for one-command local runs; 3) a small set of authored scenarios (samples/) with step assertions and a recorder that captures model/tool interactions for playback; 4) lightweight telemetry stub that emits structured events to a local file for debugging; 5) documentation pages (docs/quickstarts/mini-rag-studio.md) and a short HowTo for embedding the quickstart into demos. Backwards compatibility: the quickstart is entirely self-contained under quickstarts/mini-rag-studio and does not change global CLI commands. Security: no remote telemetry by default; all network calls are disabled in dev mode except optional feature flags. Runbook + checklist included in the description. See commits and changed files for details.

title: Add introspective RAG playground + debugger walkthrough and probe adapters
preview:
Context and motivation:
This quickstart helps engineers debug RAG pipelines end-to-end in a reproducible local environment. Teams building retrieval-augmented features have asked for better visibility into where failures and hallucinations originate (retriever, index, ranking, or model generation). The aim of this PR is to provide a small, opinionated playground that surfaces retrieval traces, prefix/KV cache snapshots, and token-level model call traces — while keeping everything runnable with a single devcontainer + smoke CI job.

What this PR adds (high-level):
- A new quickstart workspace 'rag-introspection' with README-guided walkthrough and sample corpora.
- Devcontainer configuration for instant onboarding and preinstalled tooling (Python, Node, yarn, poetry).
- Two minimal runners: a TypeScript 'playground-runner' and a Python 'play-run' that demonstrate instrumented calls to the Redwood API and a stubbed local retriever.
- Retrieval trace visualizer: a tiny static HTML/JS viewer that shows retrieval candidates, scoring, and span highlights (no external services required).
- Token-level generation trace adaptor: middleware that records per-token timing, KV-cache hits, and emitted logits (sanitized for privacy).
- Lightweight telemetry probe (YAML) and an e2e smoke test that validates the tracing pipeline.
- Docs site update linking the new quickstart under /docs/quickstarts/rag-introspection and a compact troubleshooting section.

Why this approach:
- Focused visibility: we purposely keep the example small and instrumented so teams can copy the adapters into their Dedicated or Private deployments.
- Non-invasive: runtime adapters are designed as shims that sit between Redwood SDK calls and the model client — they can be toggled in production.
- Reproducibility: devcontainer + smoke CI ensures the quickstart works cross-platform and is maintained by CI.

Checklist (completed):
- [x] Add quickstart scaffold and README walkthrough
- [x] Implement TS and Python runners with instrumentation hooks
- [x] Add retrieval trace visualizer and sample index
- [x] Add telemetry probe + smoke CI job
- [x] Update docs site with walk-through and troubleshooting
- [x] Add unit/integration smoke tests and CI guard

Testing performed:
- Local devcontainer run: executed the full walkthrough (indexing sample docs, running TS runner, verifying trace output).
- CI smoke job: runs in GitHub Actions against a mocked Redwood API endpoint; traces and telemetry are validated.
- Manual cross-check: ensured the adapters do not leak raw documents or PII; all traces are redacted by default.

Related links and references:
- Linear: ENG-4212 (RAG visibility tools), PM-88 (quickstart priority)
- Docs target: /docs/quickstarts/rag-introspection (new page)
- Example runners: examples/rag-introspection/ts-runner and examples/rag-introspection/python-runner

Notes for reviewers:
- The core instrumentation is intentionally small and well-documented in the README — please verify the privacy/redaction section.
- The retrieval visualizer is a static artifact (no backend) to make it safe for demos.
- If you want to test with a live model, set the environment variable PLAYGROUND_USE_REMOTE_MODEL=true; otherwise the runners use the built-in mock model for deterministic traces.

Commits (summary):
- feat(rag-introspection): add scaffold, README, and sample corpus
- feat(runner): add TypeScript runner with tracing middleware
- feat(runner): add Python runner and example notebook
- feat(visualizer): add retrieval trace visualizer (static HTML/JS)
- feat(telemetry): add probe yaml and CI smoke job
- docs: add quickstart page and troubleshooting section

Merge notes:
- CI passed after one re-run of the smoke job (flaky network on first run).
- Squash-merged to main after approvals.

title: Introduce snapshot-branch authoring quickstart for iterative RAG scenario branching
preview:
Problem: teams authoring RAG scenarios frequently need to iterate variations of prompts, tool bindings, and small index changes in isolation and then merge the best variants back into a canonical scenario. Existing quickstarts show single-file examples or full-playground runners but lack a lightweight, reproducible branching workflow for local authoring and telemetry-driven selection.

What this PR adds: a Snapshot-Branch Quickstart that provides a simple authoring loop: capture a snapshot of the current scenario, create a branch, run iterative edits (prompts, tool contracts, index seed), record deterministic telemetry for each run, and merge a chosen snapshot back into the main scenario. The quickstart is intentionally minimal: no server required, runs locally with the Redwood CLI and the quickstart runner, and includes a devcontainer for reproducible environments.

Key features: snapshot and branch metadata format (YAML + JSON), a tiny runner that replays scenarios deterministically (samples seeded), stepwise telemetry presets for comparing outputs, an example branching workflow (create/compare/merge), devcontainer + smoke CI for reproducibility, and docsite pages with guidance on evaluation criteria.

Usage summary: 1) git clone; 2) open devcontainer or run ./scripts/bootstrap.sh; 3) redwood quickstart run --snapshot create --name=baseline; 4) edit quickstarts/snap-branch/scenario.yaml to try a prompt variant; 5) redwood quickstart run --snapshot branch --from=baseline --name=variant-A; 6) run ./scripts/compare_snapshots.py --left=baseline --right=variant-A to produce a concise diff and telemetry summary; 7) merge with redwood quickstart snapshot merge --source=variant-A --target=baseline if chosen.

Compatibility: works with Redwood CLI (v1.8+), uses the local-hosted test runner (no cloud account required). The quickstart is designed to be safe for enterprise (no secret material stored); optional telemetry uploads are gated behind explicit consent.

Testing and CI: included small e2e smoke test that runs two snapshot branches and asserts stable determinism. CI workflow runs on push and verifies devcontainer build + smoke e2e.

Acceptance criteria: documentation page added, quickstart runner script with tests, devcontainer recipe, telemetry presets file, and a compact example scenario demonstrating branching.

Related: ENG-2104 (authoring UX), DOC-472 (quickstart doc site slot).

title: Introduce stepwise RAG authoring kiosk and Go agent examples with telemetry probes
preview: This PR adds a compact stepwise authoring "kiosk" quickstart for building RAG workflows and a minimal Go-based example agent that demonstrates tool-calling, structured output, and telemetry probes. Motivation: provide a low-friction, reproducible onboarding path for engineers who want to prototype multi-step retrieval workflows locally and validate them with lightweight step assertions. The kiosk is intentionally opinionated: it exposes a guided step sequencer, live index preview, and a watch-mode that mirrors edits to a local authoring file. The Go sample agent demonstrates secure tool invocation patterns, streaming handling, and a small telemetry shim that emits step-level events compatible with Redwood Console. Checklist: - [x] Add stepwise authoring UI quickstart with README, run instructions, and configuration templates - [x] Add Go sample agent with tool stubs and example tests - [x] Add telemetry probe YAML schema and example probe rules - [x] Integrate smoke CI job for quickstart validation - [x] Provide migration notes and compatibility guidance (no breaking API changes) How to run locally: 1) git clone the repo and cd quickstarts/rag-kiosk 2) docker-compose up --build to start the kiosk (or run the dev script for watch mode) 3) in another shell, cd golang/sample-agent && go run ./cmd/agent to start the example agent 4) follow the README-guided scenario to step through a retrieval + tool call sequence Notes on telemetry: the probes/ directory contains small example YAML rules that assert presence/ordering of events (step.enter, step.exit, tool.call). These are intentionally pluggable; customers can adapt them to Redwood Console ingestion. CI and test strategy: added a lightweight smoke job runs the kiosk in headless mode and exercises the Go agent over localhost; tests are intentionally small to keep CI latency low. Rollout and compatibility: this is an additive quickstart; it does not modify existing APIs or infra. Recommended canary: ship as preview docs to docs.redwood.ai and monitor telemetry event rates for the sample scenario. See release_notes for user-facing guidance.

title: Add RAG Orchestration Smokehouse walkthrough and reproducible smoke CI
preview:
Motivation: provide a compact, reproducible authoring + test walkthrough for building RAG-based orchestrations that call external tools. Many teams ask for a single, batteries-included example that covers: index seeding, simple tool-binding (HTTP webhook + local mock), stepwise agent execution, and a smoke CI that can run locally or in CI to catch regressions to tool-calling and structured output.

What this PR adds (summary):
- A lightweight ‘smokehouse’ quickstart under quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/ that includes: README, seed index script, tiny in-process tool-mock server, example orchestration YAML, and an idempotent smoke CI runner.
- Two example agent patterns: stepwise-orchestrator and event-driven-saga (small, focused code + config in TypeScript).
- A reproducible smoke CI harness (node-based) that runs against a pinned dev model variant and asserts structured outputs for three core scenarios (faq lookup, invoice-tool-call, and multi-step refinement).

Commits (high-level):
- feat: add smokehouse directory with README and starter templates (Ava Chen)
- feat: implement local tool-mock server and CI runner (Ava Chen)
- docs: walkthrough with troubleshooting, telemetry knobs, and rollout guidance (Priya Nair)
- test: add deterministic fixtures + e2e smoke assertions (Diego Morales)

Files changed (high-level):
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/README.md  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/seed_index.py  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/tool-mock/server.ts  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/examples/stepwise-orch.ts  (new)

title: Introduce RAG rehearsal sandbox with step-mocks and eval hooks
preview:
Motivation: Add a small, local-first RAG "rehearsal" quickstart that helps engineers and evaluators iterate on end-to-end scenarios deterministically. The sandbox provides: 1) a scenario YAML format for stepwise interactions (queries, tool calls, agent decisions), 2) step-mock harnesses to replace external tools (search, embeddings, vector stores) with canned responses, 3) a lightweight CLI runner to replay scenarios locally and capture structured traces, and 4) eval hooks that emit run-level metrics and Golden-expected assertions for CI integration.

This PR introduces the example project, docs, and a test harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Add quickstart scaffolding and README
- [x] Implement scenario schema and runner CLI
- [x] Provide three sample scenarios (happy path, ambiguous retrieval, safety-fallback)
- [x] Add step-mock implementations for search/knn/embeddings
- [x] Integrate telemetry hooks for trace export (jsonl)
- [x] Wire up a smoke test in CI
- [x] Update quickstarts index and docs sidebar

Usage summary: After checkout run `./bin/rag-rehearse run scenarios/happy-path.yml --mock-store=fixtures/mock_store.json --out=out/happy-path.json`

The runner will output a structured trace of prompt fragments, tool calls, model responses, and evaluation assertions. This makes it easy to reproduce failures reported by customers or caught by automated evals.

Commits:
- initial scaffold: add quickstart directory, README, license note
- add scenario schema and parser
- implement CLI runner and trace exporter
- add three example scenarios and fixtures
- add CI smoke job and docs index entry

Changed files (high level):
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/README.md
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/bin/rehearse (executable runner script)
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/schema/scenario.yaml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/happy-path.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/ambiguous-retrieval.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/safety-fallback.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/fixtures/mock_store.json
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/hooks/telemetry_exporter.py
- docs/quickstarts-index.yml (added entry)

Tests & CI: Added a quick smoke job in .github/workflows/rehearse-smoke.yml which runs the runner against the three included scenarios and asserts that the exported traces contain expected assertion tags. CI runs: lint (pass), unit (pass), smoke (pass).

Review conversation summary:
- Diego: "Nice minimal surface, helps reproduce a lot of postmortems. Could we make the schema optional fields explicit?" Author reply: "Added more explicit field docs and defaults in schema and README (see commit 3)."
- Ava: "CLI UX looks good. Please ensure the mock_store format is compatible with existing vector-store fixtures." Author reply: "Adjusted fixture format to match vector-store shim used in other quickstarts; added conversion helper in fixtures/convert_mock.py."
- Liam: "Telemetry export should be opt-in; don't log PII by default." Author reply: "Telemetry exporter defaults to local jsonl file and strips prompt secrets; added note in README and env var to toggle enrichment."

Additional notes:
- The scenario schema is intentionally compact and opinionated to keep the quickstart approachable; teams can extend it for more complex orchestration.
- The step-mock harness uses a priority-matching rule set so teams can create partial-match fixtures for fuzzier retrieval tests.
- Release notes: "Add RAG rehearsal sandbox quickstart: local runner, scenario schema, step-mocks, and telemetry hooks. Useful for reproducible debugging and automated evals."

Migration/compatibility: "No breaking changes. This is an additive quickstart and does not change runtime or SDK behavior."

title: introduce-rag-llm-bridge-authoring-slate-quickstart
preview:
Context and motivation: Teams building retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workflows often need a minimal, opinionated authoring surface that ties together prompt templates, tool contracts, structured output validators, and live telemetry presets. This quickstart ("LLM Bridge Authoring Slate"

What this PR adds: a compact developer playground and recipe that demonstrates: 1) contract-first tool declarations (OpenAPI/JSON Schema style) that the RAG runtime can call safely; 2) a lightweight authoring slate UI for editing prompt templates and live-previewing structured outputs; 3) a small sample indexer and retrieval shim (sqlite + simple vectorizer) for local experiments; 4) telemetry presets (token/response latency breakdown, tool-call traces, schema validation failures) wired to the Console dev-mode; and 5) a step-by-step README and end-to-end smoke tests for CI.

Why this is different: instead of a broad feature-complete sample, this quickstart focuses on choreography: how prompt variants, tool contracts, and structured-output validators interact during authoring. It intentionally keeps infra minimal so engineers can iterate locally and port patterns into Dedicated/Private deployments.

Checklist (author):
- [x] add quickstart skeleton under quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate (README, sample code, UI stubs)
- [x] implement contract-first tool adapter and example connector (tools/email-send.json, tools/search-contract.json)
- [x] add structured-output linter and example schema (schemas/action-trace.json)
- [x] wire minimal telemetry probes and Console integration guide
- [x] add smoke e2e test (ci/smoke-llm-bridge.yml) and basic unit tests for the retrieval shim

Summary of commits:
- init: add llm-bridge-slate quickstart skeleton and README
- feat: contract-first tool adapter + example contracts
- feat: authoring slate UI (static devpage) and preview server
- test: add smoke CI workflow and unit tests for retrieval shim
- docs: update quickstarts index and add Console dev-mode integration instructions

Changed areas/files (high-level):
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/README.md (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/app/ (new: small express/fastapi style preview server, UI assets)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/tools/search-contract.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/tools/email-send.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/schemas/action-trace.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/telemetry-presets.json (new)
- ci/smoke-llm-bridge.yml (new)
- docs/quickstarts/index.md (tweaked)

Small illustrative snippet added to the tool adapter (contract-first): tools/search-contract.json shows a description and a JSON Schema for the expected inputs; caller-side glue validates the response against schemas/action-trace.json before emitting traces to telemetry. No production credentials or secrets are included.

Review conversation highlights:
- Samir: "Nice focused surface — can we make the schema failure path more visible in the Console preset?"
- Maya: "Added an explicit 'schema-failure' trace type and an example in telemetry-presets.json; updated README to show how to toggle dev-mode traces."
- Joanna: "CI was flaky on the first run due to sqlite lock on macos-latest runner. Consider a retry/backoff."
- Maya: "Added lock-and-retry to the retrieval shim init and bumped the timeout in the smoke workflow."
- Ethan: "Docs wording looks good, but let's ensure we reference the runtime routing policy when describing canary-to-dedicated transitions."
- Maya: "Added a note under 'porting to Dedicated' section linking to runtime policies doc (docs/routing.md)."

CI and checks: First CI run failed on the smoke e2e due to sqlite concurrency, rerun passed. Linting and unit tests pass. The smoke workflow is conservative (runs on push to feature branch) and gated behind a quickstart label for the repository.

Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge after approvals from Samir and Joanna and green CI.

Usage and next steps: The README contains a local quickstart (npm run dev / python preview_server.py), a guided exercise to edit the tool contract and see the structured-output lint fire, and a short recipe for wiring the quickstart to a Redwood hosted API key for developers who want to test real model calls. Future follow-ups (tracked in ENG-7192): Dockerized dev container for offline authoring, additional example connectors (calendar, github-issue), and a Canary > Dedicated migration note with concrete config examples.

title: introduce-stateful-rag-finite-state-machine-quickstart
preview: Context & motivation: Many teams want RAG flows that maintain explicit conversational state across turns (session phases, latent tool state, multi-step confirmations) without shipping a heavyweight orchestrator. This PR introduces a compact Quickstart that wires a finite-state-machine (FSM) pattern to Redwood's tool-calling and incremental index APIs so developers can bootstrap stateful RAG sessions with clear guardrails and reproducible telemetry. Summary of changes: 1) new quickstart: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/ containing a README, TypeScript sample runner, an auth-free local dev shim, and a test harness; 2) authoring doc: docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md with design rationale and recommended prompt templates; 3) telemetry presets: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/telemetry-presets.json for Console probe ingestion; 4) smoke CI workflow: .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml to validate local run in CI; 5) small SDK helper: packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts (new lightweight FSM utilities) 6) tests: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts High-level commit summary: - Initial quickstart scaffold and README. - Add FSM runtime helpers and inline JSDoc + types. - Add authoring guide with example prompts and guardrails. - Add telemetry presets and console link example. - Add GitHub smoke workflow and a minimal unit test. Changed areas/files (high level): quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/{README.md,runner.ts,telemetry-presets.json,__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts}, docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md, packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts, .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml Notable design points: - FSM model: explicit states (INIT, RETRIEVE, TOOL_CALL, CONFIRM, FINALIZE) and deterministic transitions on structured outputs. - Tool binding: demonstrates idempotent tool wrappers and a replay-safe invocation pattern using request-scoped tokens. - Caching: shows prefix KV caching for retrieval tokens between RETRIEVE->TOOL_CALL transitions to reduce repeated embedding calls. - Telemetry: emits per-state timers and transition tags consumed by Redwood Console via provided presets. Single-line summary of example runner behavior: The TS runner seeds an in-memory index, starts a session in INIT, emits a retrieval request, enters RETRIEVE, chooses candidate passages, optionally enters TOOL_CALL (e.g., call external knowledge API or custom search tool), moves to CONFIRM when candidate requires human-in-loop confirmation, and then FINALIZE to return structured output; the runner includes a --simulate-human flag for deterministic CI. Review conversation (high-level): Diego: "Please add an example of error path transition when retrieval returns empty; show fallback to open-domain generation." Author (Maya): "Added fallback transition with explicit prompt template and covered in authoring doc." Priya: "Telemetry presets look good; please ensure tags use kebab-case." Author: "Fixed tags and added unit test asserting telemetry fields." Samir (approval): "Works for me, tests and CI pass." CI checks: lint (pass), unit-tests (pass), smoke-ci-run (pass after one rerun for environment flakiness), docs-build (pass). Testing notes: smoke CI uses node 18 and a lightweight in-memory vector index, tests assert deterministic outputs using seeded RNG. Migration/compat notes: SDK helper fsm.ts is additive and exported from packages/sdk-utils index without breaking existing APIs. Rollout plan: ship as a Quickstart under the Quickstarts landing page with a short blurb and a linked authoring guide. Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge after approvals. How to review locally: 1) cd quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag 2) yarn install && yarn build 3) yarn run seed && yarn run start -- --simulate-human 4) run the smoke test: yarn test:fsm-smoke Why this is different from other RAG quickstarts: most quickstarts focus on tool-calling, debugging, or replay; this one centers an explicit FSM pattern and shows best practices for state transitions, telemetry tags per state, idempotent tool wrappers, and KV-backed retrieval caching. Related linear/Jira: ENG-2103 (FSM runtime utility), PM-812 (Quickstart deliverable). Checklist: [x] Add quickstart scaffold [x] Add authoring guide [x] Add telemetry presets [x] Add smoke CI [x] Add unit tests [x] Update packages/sdk-utils export [x] Ensure docs build clean [x] Add release notes entry Post-merge follow-ups: - Add a short walkthrough video to docs site (task assigned to PM-812). - Consider adding a Go translation of the runner if there is demand. Contact: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.ai) for follow-ups and questions.

title: Add snippet-explorer worklet with typed telemetry hooks and runnable README
preview:
Motivation: shipping compact, runnable quickstarts that demonstrate structured tool-calling, streaming snippets, and minimal telemetry hooks has been a frequent ask from customer-facing teams. This PR introduces a new quickstart called 'snippet-explorer' — a small web worklet + Node runner that shows how to wire Redwood SDK streaming, tool calls, and typed telemetry probes into a developer playground.

What this PR contains: a) quickstarts/snippet-explorer/README.md with a one-minute intro, runnable steps (devcontainer, npm start, and a single smoke request). b) playlet/index.ts: lightweight worklet that runs a sample agent, exposes a compact UI stub and emits typed probe events. c) examples/snippet-explorer/telemetry-probes.yaml: example probe schema and recommended labels for integration with Console ingestion. d) docs/quickstarts/snippet-explorer.md: docs-site-ready copy with API snippets and common troubleshooting. e) ci/smoke-tests/snippet-explorer.yml: minimal CI smoke to ensure startup and sample request succeed in CI. f) devcontainer.json and package.json updates to make local onboarding one-command.

Design notes and tradeoffs: the worklet intentionally keeps dependencies minimal (no browser UI framework) so teams can copy-paste into embedded sandboxes. Telemetry hooks are typed (TypeScript interfaces) and intentionally shallow: event types: session.start, request.kv_cache_hit, tool.call, render.fragment. We opted to emit JSON-serializable objects only (no binary payloads) to minimize console-side ingestion complexity.

Usage instructions (excerpted from README): 1) git clone && cd quickstarts/snippet-explorer 2) code . (opens devcontainer) 3) npm install && npm run dev 4) open http://localhost:5173 and press 'Run sample' to see streaming tokens and a single tool call. The README also describes environment variables used by the quickstart (REDWOOD_API_KEY, REDWOOD_REGION) and fallbacks for local dev (embedded stub tokens).

Testing and CI: smoke test runs the devserver in headless mode and sends a single sample request verifying: (1) streaming returns at least one delta, (2) the expected tool call was invoked (mock), (3) telemetry probe for session.start and tool.call were emitted to a local file sink. CI passes are reported as part of this PR.

Backward compatibility and rollout: this is an additive quickstart — no breaking change to existing quickstarts. Consumers can import the typed probe interfaces from the example package for local testing; we do not publish a new package.

Follow-ups: 1) Integrate a Console dashboard template that imports examples/snippet-explorer/telemetry-probes.yaml (tracked in ENG-1045). 2) Add a variant that uses Dedicated autoscaling example (ENG-1070).

Checklist: - [x] README and docs copy - [x] Runnable devcontainer + npm scripts - [x] Telemetry probe schema and typed hooks - [x] Minimal CI smoke - [x] Reviewer feedback addressed

Related: Linear ENG-1023 (quickstart backlog), PM-78 (customer ask).

Commits summary: see commits field for high-level commit messages.

Backward compatibility and rollout: this is an additive quickstart — no breaking change to existing quickstarts. Consumers can import the typed probe interfaces from the example package for local testing; we do not publish a new package.

Related: Linear ENG-1023 (quickstart backlog), PM-78 (customer ask).

title: Add RAG telemetry playback minirunner quickstart and docs
preview:
Motivation: Reproducible debugging of RAG sessions requires the ability to replay request/response and telemetry traces locally. This quickstart introduces a lightweight "minirunner" that replays recorded telemetry and request traces into a local RAG dev environment so engineers and SREs can reproduce quality regressions, latency spikes, and tool-calling failures without needing a full cluster.

What this PR adds: a) a minimal Go-based playback worker (examples/playback-minirunner/) that consumes Redwood-formatted trace bundles and emits the same sequence of API calls and tool invocations to a developer's local stack; b) a small TypeScript shim (examples/ts-playback-proxy/) that simulates the tool adapter surface for browser examples; c) a step-by-step quickstart (quickstarts/rag-telemetry-playback/README.md) with sample trace bundles, CLI usage, and troubleshooting tips; d) docs site snippet (docs/quickstarts/rag-telemetry-playback.md) and a live example reference under examples/sample-traces/.

Design notes: the minirunner is intentionally opinionated and small: deterministic replay (timestamp-preserving or accelerated modes), configurable injection of network latency and token-level delays, deterministic seeding for RNG-affecting model calls, and an assertions harness that allows authors to attach simple post-replay checks for expected outputs. Playback bundles are newline-delimited JSON events (NDJSON) matching our telemetry export format; the runner supports compressed (.ndjson.gz) bundles out of the box. The TS proxy is a deliberate high-level stub to make it trivial to wire browser-based examples into the same replay harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement Go minirunner with replay and latency simulation
- [x] TS proxy and two sample adapters (tool-echo, external-search-stub)
- [x] Quickstart README and stepwise tutorial with screenshots
- [x] Docs site quickstart page + sidebar entry
- [x] Unit tests for replay ordering and RNG seeding
- [x] CI integration and a small e2e smoke test using sample bundle

Compatibility: the runner targets the Redwood telemetry v1 schema; it will gracefully reject older bundles with a helpful error message and link to the migration guide. It does not depend on private infra and is safe to run on developer machines.

How to try locally (short): git checkout HEAD && cd examples/playback-minirunner && make build && ./playback-minirunner --bundle ../../examples/sample-traces/simple-rag-session.ndjson.gz --target http://localhost:8080 --accelerate 4x --assert assertions.yml

Related work: this addresses recurring developer pain surfaced in ENG-1723 and pairs with the rag session recorder introduced earlier. It will be useful for on-call reproduction, authoring regression tests, and for documentation examples that show exact event flows.

Merge notes: squash-and-merge after approvals. CI had one intermittent test failure (telemetry/serializer) on the first run; that was fixed by normalizing timestamp parsing in commit 3.

title: Introduce RAG Navigator: lightweight interactive playground and CLI walkthrough
preview: Context and motivation: teams keep asking for a tiny, batteries-included RAG playground that lets new engineers run a retrieval+agent loop locally with minimal setup and observable telemetry. This PR adds 'RAG Navigator', a lightweight quickstart that combines: 1) a minimal Node-based CLI to scaffold an index and seed prompts; 2) a small devserver that serves a compact interactive UI for stepwise inspection of agent decisions and tool calls; 3) opinionated telemetry probes that emit structured traces (local file/console) for step-level assertions; 4) example prompt templates and a guided walkthrough README. Checklist: - [x] scaffold CLI and devserver - [x] add telemetry probes and sample assertions - [x] provide two example corpora (kb-small, kb-news) - [x] include CI smoke tests and offline index fixture - [x] add docs quickstart page and links to examples gallery Implementation notes: the CLI is intentionally minimal (no auth required) and targets local-first experimentation; it supports `nav init`, `nav index --corpus=kb-small`, `nav run --scenario=tutor`, and `nav export-session`. The devserver runs on :5173, serves a compact single-page authoring UI with stepwise trace view and a tool-call inspector. Telemetry format is newline-delimited JSON to simplify ingestion by local eval harnesses. Security: playground is explicitly for dev use; README warns about not exposing to public networks. Related work: follows conventions from the authoring-studio and step-replayer projects but is intentionally compact for workshops (see ENG-4821). Testing: added a CI job that runs `nav index` against a bundled offline fixture and executes a headless smoke-run that asserts the agent reaches an expected final step. Migration and rollout: non-destructive; added under examples with its own README and docsite quickstart. Links: linear/ENG-4821, support ticket SUP-7789.

title: add-interactive-rag-kata-telemetry-workbench-and-authoring-sandbox-quickstart
preview:
Context: New developer-onboarding quickstart to accelerate RAG authoring and telemetry adoption. The goal is to provide a compact, hands-on sandbox with stepwise exercises (katas) that teach: setting up a local index, wiring a retrieval tool, authoring prompts with structured output checks, and instrumenting telemetry probes for latency/cost/quality. This PR delivers the sandbox app, three kata scenarios, a telemetry probes package, docs site updates, and CI smoke tests.

Motivation: Engineering teams repeatedly ask for reproducible, minimal examples focused on both correctness (assertions/step checks) and observability. Existing quickstarts teach authoring but omit live telemetry wiring or stepwise kata-style learning. This PR fills that gap and provides a low-friction path to try telemetry workbench features paired with RAG.

Checklist: - [x] Add interactive sandbox app with live-reload (local dev container) - [x] Add three kata scenarios (indexing, tool-calling, structured-output assertions) - [x] Add telemetry probe definitions and Console workbench integration - [x] Add lightweight e2e smoke tests + CI job - [x] Docs: quickstart page, inline walkthrough, and screenshots - [x] Add examples for Python and TypeScript client usage - [x] Add short authoring exercises and validation harness

What changed (high level): commits summarize below. Key deliverables: a new quickstart under quickstarts/rag-kata, telemetry/probes/kata-probe.yaml, docs/quickstarts/rag-kata.md, authoring-sandbox web app (React + local shim), and CI smoke workflow ci/smoke/rag-kata-smoke.yml. Files changed (high level): quickstarts/rag-kata/README.md; quickstarts/rag-kata/devcontainer.json; quickstarts/rag-kata/sandbox/Dockerfile; quickstarts/rag-kata/sandbox/src/*; quickstarts/rag-kata/cli/rag-kata (executable stub); docs/quickstarts/rag-kata.md; telemetry/probes/kata-probe.yaml; examples/rag-kata-scenarios/{index.json,prompts.json,assertions.json}; ci/smoke/rag-kata-smoke.yml; tests/e2e/rag-kata.spec.ts; package.json updates for authoring-sandbox.

Review conversation (summary): - Claire: requested clearer failure messages for step assertions and a small refactor to the telemetry probe labels for dashboard filtering. - Priya: addressed with commit "refactor: normalize probe labels for dashboard" and updated docs with examples. - Diego: asked for an e2e smoke job and a compact CI matrix (node12/node16) — added CI job and pinned a single Node version to reduce flakiness. - Ethan: suggested sample prompts to demonstrate tool-fallback behavior and added an additional kata to exercise fallback. title: Add RAG tool-bound workflow quickstart with stubbed agent runtime
preview: Motivation: provide a small, battery-included quickstart for teams authoring retrieval-augmented tool-calling agents that need fast local feedback loops. This quickstart packages: 1) a tiny stubbed agent runtime (TypeScript) that runs locally and exposes a tool-binding API; 2) a set of contract-first tool schemas and structured-output validators; 3) an incremental index + mocked retriever that can be swapped for a real vector DB; 4) telemetry probe scaffolds and a smoke test harness for CI. The goal is to let engineers iterate on tool contracts and prompt/tool orchestration without provisioning full infra or real connectors. What this PR changes: - Adds quickstarts/toolbound-agent/README.md with step-by-step authoring, troubleshooting, and intended learning objectives. - Adds an opinionated example agent at quickstarts/toolbound-agent/examples/agent.ts demonstrating tool discovery, deterministic replay, and partial-response resume. - Adds a compact mock retriever and incremental indexer (quickstarts/toolbound-agent/lib/indexer.ts) and a CLI (dev/runner.ts) to run scenarios offline. - Adds structured output schema examples (schemas/tool-contracts.json) with a lightweight validator and linter integration. - Adds CI smoke pipeline config (.github/workflows/quickstart-ci.yml) that runs headless scenario replay and the structured-output linter. - Adds telemetry probe presets (telemetry/probes.yaml) and docs page (docs/rag-toolbound.md) describing recommended metrics and alert thresholds. Tests and verification: ran the local runner across three seed scenarios (happy path, tool-misuse, partial response resume) and exercised the structured-output linter. CI: all checks pass in the quickstart pipeline (smoke tests + linter). Checklist: - [x] Quickstart README with usage and learning objectives - [x] Example agent demonstrating tool-binding and structured-output - [x] Mock retriever and incremental index for offline iteration - [x] Structured-output schema + lint integration - [x] CI smoke workflow for reproducible validation - [x] Telemetry probe presets and docs link Testing notes and tips: runner seeds are intentionally small (≤ 3 documents) to keep latency low. To integrate with a real index swap the 'MockRetriever' in examples/agent.ts; instructions included in README. Related work: this quickstart is intentionally narrower than the full RAG Playbook quickstarts — it complements the existing RAG templates by focusing on contract-first tool-binding authoring and offline validation. Linked Linear: ENG-4232 (authoring DX), PM-987 (quickstart intake). Merge: Squash-merged after two rounds of review and one CI re-run. Known limitations: no cloud indexer included; the runner is not production-grade and is intended for authoring/eval only. Future work: add a Rust/WASM minimal runner and a Python adapter for teams who prefer those stacks.

title: Add “Hello world” first request snippet aligned with activation CTAs
preview:
## Context
This sprint’s primary activation KPI is “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” within 7 days. We’ve seen drop-off at the earliest step due to (a) too many options in the quickstart, (b) mismatch between lifecycle email CTAs and what the docs show, and (c) missing/unclear env var and model defaults.

This PR adds a minimal, copy/paste “Hello world” snippet that matches the onboarding email v2 + console onboarding checklist CTA wording: “Make your first request.” The snippet is intentionally small: create API key → export env var → single streaming request.

## What changed
- New `hello-world` page that is optimized for TTFT (goal: <2 minutes from API key creation to first streamed token).
- Adds 3 variants with identical structure:
  - curl (streaming)
  - Node/TypeScript (SDK)
  - Python (SDK)
- Standardizes naming used across growth surfaces:
  - Env var: `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
  - Base URL: `https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1`
  - Model example: `redwood/verified-llm-small` (placeholder here is a stable, low-latency model option used in onboarding)
- Adds a small “Common errors” callout focused on the top early failures we’ve seen (missing key / 401, model typo / 404, network / proxy).
- Adds UTM-safe anchors for lifecycle email deep links and console CTA targets (no JS; just stable fragment ids).

## Example (curl)
```bash
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-llm-small",
    "stream": true,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}]
  }'
```

## Measurement / instrumentation notes
- This repo doesn’t emit product events, but the snippet is designed to correspond cleanly to funnel events being instrumented in the API/console (ENG-10492): `api_key_created` → `first_request_succeeded`.
- The page includes consistent CTA copy to reduce confusion in qualitative feedback and support tickets.

## QA
- Verified commands work end-to-end against staging.
- Ensured snippets are mobile-friendly (short lines, minimal wrapping).
- Confirmed no mention of experimental features or non-default flags.

## Rollout
- Safe to merge immediately; Growth will link to this page in lifecycle email v2 + console nudge CTAs via existing docs PRs.

## Screenshots
- N/A (markdown-only change)

title: Add RAG assertion workshop and offline eval kit
preview:
Motivation: Engineers building retrieval-augmented agents need reproducible, offline workflows to author prompt/knowledge assertions and validate regressions without spinning up hosted infra. This quickstart provides a compact workshop that demonstrates assertion-driven evals, example agent tool-calling patterns, and telemetry probes that map errors to actionable traces.

What this PR adds: - a guided workshop README with hands-on tasks and expected outcomes - a tiny assertion DSL and runner (authorable JSON/YAML assertion files) to express functional and quality checks - an offline eval harness that spins a local retriever + lightweight model shim for fast iteration - telemetry probe presets (span names, tags, and sampling hints) and a small dashboard template - CI smoke job to run the assertion suite on PRs so regressions are caught early

Key usage notes: 1) Clone the quickstart and follow the README at quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop/README.md for a 20-minute workshop. 2) To run locally: sh scripts/run-local-eval.sh --index ./data/small-corpus.json --assertions assertions/examples.yaml 3) To add assertions: author a new entry in assertions/ with description, input, expected retrieval top-k, and a fuzzy-match quality threshold. 4) Telemetry: add TELEMETRY_ENDPOINT to env and use the provided probes.yaml to map spans to workshop steps.

Checklist: - [x] Add README + step tasks - [x] Implement assertion runner and examples - [x] Add telemetry presets and dashboard skeleton - [x] Add CI smoke job and local run script - [x] Wire minimal example agent with tool-calling stub - [x] Tests: unit tests for assertion runner, end-to-end offline smoke run

Related: ENG-3921 (authoring-first evals), PM-684 (workshop materials), SUP-1205 (customer request for offline regression checks). Docs preview: https://docs.redwood.com/quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop

Notes on scope: Designed to be small and local-first — it intentionally uses a model shim to keep costs low for teams onboarding. The toolkit is extensible and includes pointers for switching to a production-backed retriever or Redwood-hosted models.
text:
Motivation: Engineers building retrieval-augmented agents need reproducible, offline workflows to author prompt/knowledge assertions and validate regressions without spinning up hosted infra. This quickstart provides a compact workshop that demonstrates assertion-driven evals, example agent tool-calling patterns, and telemetry probes that map errors to actionable traces.

What this PR adds: - a guided workshop README with hands-on tasks and expected outcomes - a tiny assertion DSL and runner (authorable JSON/YAML assertion files) to express functional and quality checks - an offline eval harness that spins a local retriever + lightweight model shim for fast iteration - telemetry probe presets (span names, tags, and sampling hints) and a small dashboard template - CI smoke job to run the assertion suite on PRs so regressions are caught early

Key usage notes: 1) Clone the quickstart and follow the README at quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop/README.md for a 20-minute workshop. 2) To run locally: sh scripts/run-local-eval.sh --index ./data/small-corpus.json --assertions assertions/examples.yaml 3) To add assertions: author a new entry in assertions/ with description, input, expected retrieval top-k, and a fuzzy-match quality threshold. 4) Telemetry: add TELEMETRY_ENDPOINT to env and use the provided probes.yaml to map spans to workshop steps.

Checklist: - [x] Add README + step tasks - [x] Implement assertion runner and examples - [x] Add telemetry presets and dashboard skeleton - [x] Add CI smoke job and local run script - [x] Wire minimal example agent with tool-calling stub - [x] Tests: unit tests for assertion runner, end-to-end offline smoke run

Related: ENG-3921 (authoring-first evals), PM-684 (workshop materials), SUP-1205 (customer request for offline regression checks). Docs preview: https://docs.redwood.com/quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop

Notes on scope: Designed to be small and local-first — it intentionally uses a model shim to keep costs low for teams onboarding. The toolkit is extensible and includes pointers for switching to a production-backed retriever or Redwood-hosted models.

title: interactive rag scenarios: tool timeouts and resilient fallbacks quickstart
preview: Adds an interactive quickstart demonstrating robust RAG agent patterns when calling external tools: per-tool timeout and retry policies, deterministic fallback strategies (local cache / degraded-mode answers), session replay for authoring and debugging, and lightweight telemetry probes for latency-sensitive deployments. The quickstart includes a compact dev server to run scenarios locally, stepwise assertions for authoring test cases, and CI smoke tests that validate timeouts and fallback behavior. Motivated by ENG-1843 and on-call feedback about tool stalls increasing MTTR; this quickstart is aimed at engineers evaluating production guardrails for tool-calling agents and improves observability during local dev and CI verification.

title: Introduce RAG step-factory playbook and lightweight e2e devkit
preview: Context and motivation: Teams repeatedly ask for a compact, production-adjacent RAG quickstart that (1) defines reusable step templates for common pipeline stages (query rewrite, candidate fetch, rerank, synthesis), (2) ships a tiny devkit for deterministic e2e runs and replayable telemetry, and (3) demonstrates a connector pattern (GraphQL) used in real products. This PR introduces the 'step-factory' playbook: a curated set of YAML step templates, a node-based devkit runner with a lightweight in-memory index/replay harness, and docs + smoke CI to make authoring and testing RAG flows straightforward. What this contains (high level): - playbooks/step-factory/README.md with conceptual overview and authoring patterns - playbooks/step-factory/templates/* (5 canonical step templates: rewrite, retrieve, rerank, synthesize, commit) - devkit/runner/index.ts: small runner that composes steps, exposes a CLI (rw-step run --playbook playbooks/step-factory/demo.yaml) - devkit/replay/probes.ts: deterministic replay harness and step-level assertion helpers - examples/graphql-connector/sample-connector.ts: example adapter showing connector->tool-call pattern and auth stub - docs/site/content/quickstarts/step-factory.md: full quickstart for docs site with config snippets and troubleshooting - tests/e2e/step-factory.spec.ts: end-to-end smoke with seeded index and step assertions - ci/workflows/quickstart-smoke.yml: nightly smoke and PR run (fast mode) Implementation notes: - Step templates are small YAML fragments (no infra assumptions). They reference minimal tool contracts (input/outputs) so teams can adapt them to Dedicated/Private deploys. - The devkit runner intentionally avoids heavy deps; it supports local in-memory corpora and a simple HTTP-based mock connector for external tools. - Replay harness records step inputs/outputs and asserts invariants (token budget, latency upper bound, structured-output schemas). - Example GraphQL connector demonstrates incremental indexing and delta-sync semantics for demo data sets. Migration and compatibility: - Non-breaking: templates are additive and the runner is opt-in. - If teams adopt the runner, they may want to replace the in-memory index with their existing vector store; the README includes migration pointers. Checklist (what I did): - [x] Add playbook templates and demo playbook - [x] Implement lightweight devkit runner + replay probes - [x] Add example GraphQL connector and auth stub - [x] Docs quickstart page + local CLI usage - [x] E2E smoke tests and CI workflow - [x] Add linear links and release notes Drafting notes: Linear ENG-8642 contains the design doc and acceptance criteria. See demo recording link (internal docs) in the Linear ticket. This description is intentionally long to aid reviewers and release notes.

title: Add production readiness quickstart aligned with Console onboarding checklist
preview: This adds a new end-to-end “Production Readiness” quickstart intended to mirror the Console onboarding / first production launch checklist. The quickstart walks a new Hosted API user from: API key setup → first request → streaming → retries/timeouts → usage visibility → quotas/budgets guardrails → basic observability hooks → model pinning + fallback (conceptual; uses config placeholders). Motivation: we keep seeing early prod launches fail on 401/403 setup mistakes, streaming disconnect confusion, and 429 surprises; the checklist in Console helps, but we need a canonical runnable path customers can copy/paste and deploy. Scope: adds a new quickstart directory with Python + TypeScript examples (Go is stubbed with TODO and links to the SDK guide), adds a minimal “healthcheck” endpoint pattern, structured logs, and a tiny dashboard note for usage-lag expectations. Includes guidance for where to configure quotas/budgets in Console and links out to Terraform sample budget alerts. Non-goals: full guided tour, Dedicated/Private specifics, deep integration with the checklist progress service (this is docs/examples only).

title: Add optional telemetry tags/headers for template adoption metrics
preview:
## Context
Product (PM-912) needs a reliable way to identify template-originated traffic for adoption KPIs (activation funnel, time-to-first-success, support deflection). Today, requests generated by the quickstarts are indistinguishable from hand-rolled integrations once users copy/paste code.

We want a convention that:
- Is **opt-in** (some users run behind strict corporate proxies / header allowlists)
- Is **privacy-safe** (no user identifiers, no workspace/customer IDs, no prompt content)
- Has **low-cardinality** values to avoid metrics blowups
- Works across **Python + TypeScript templates** and local dev/prod deployments
- Is visible in logs/traces for support debugging, but doesn’t require custom instrumentation

## What this PR does
Adds an optional telemetry tagging convention used by quickstart templates when calling the Redwood API:
- **Header:** `X-Redwood-Template` (string)
- **Value format:** `<template_id>@<template_version>`
  - Example: `rag-agent-python@0.3.0`
  - `template_id` is a short, static identifier for the template (no user input)
  - `template_version` is the template release version embedded at scaffold time

Also adds an optional trace/log attribute so support can correlate issues when users paste logs:
- **OTel attribute (client span):** `redwood.template` = same value as above

### Opt-in behavior
Telemetry tagging is **disabled by default**. Templates only attach `X-Redwood-Template` when `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=1` (or `true`).

Rationale: we’ve seen corporate proxies reject unknown headers, and we don’t want templates to fail “mysteriously” on first run for users in locked-down environments.

### Redaction / privacy
- The header value is constructed exclusively from **template code constants** and the embedded template version.
- No environment variables (except the boolean enable flag) are included.
- No hostnames, org IDs, user IDs, or prompt/tool payloads are included.

## Implementation details
- Introduces a small shared helper in `packages/common/telemetry_tag.ts` and `packages/common/telemetry_tag.py` (mirrored structure across languages) that:
  - Parses `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY`
  - Produces the tag string
  - Applies it to Redwood client request options
- Stamps template version in the scaffolded app via `template.manifest.json` (already present from CLI init) and uses it as the version component.
- Updates both Python and TypeScript RAG+agent templates to plumb the tag into the Redwood client initialization.

## Example (TypeScript)
If enabled:
- Env: `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=1`
- Outbound request includes: `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent-ts@0.3.0`

## Example (Python)
If enabled:
- Env: `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=true`
- Outbound request includes: `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent-python@0.3.0`

## Tests
- Unit tests for env parsing (accepts `1/0`, `true/false`, case-insensitive)
- Unit tests for formatting and allowed characters
- Smoke test ensures requests succeed with tagging enabled/disabled (no behavioral change on API responses)

## Notes / follow-ups
- Gateway needs to allowlist the header for hosted users. That change is tracked separately in the main `redwood` monorepo.
- If we see continued proxy issues, we can add an alternate mechanism (query param) but prefer header to avoid accidental propagation into logs at intermediaries.

## Checklist
- [x] Opt-in default off
- [x] Low-cardinality tag values
- [x] No user-controlled tag content
- [x] Documented in template README + troubleshooting
- [x] Tests added

title: Pin template dependencies + add vulnerability scanning workflows
preview: Summary: This PR locks down dependency versions across the Python and TypeScript quickstart templates, adds automated vulnerability scanning in CI (PR-time + scheduled), and documents the upgrade process for template maintainers to reduce supply-chain risk. Motivation: Security review flagged unpinned transitive dependencies and lack of automated scanning (see ENG-4190 and template security review open items). We also want template repos to adhere to the Secure SDLC addendum for templates (dependency pinning, vulnerability scanning, and a repeatable upgrade story) without introducing noisy/flake-prone CI failures. Changes included: (1) Python template: move to fully pinned requirements (direct + transitive) via pip-tools, commit compiled requirements with hashes, and add a make target to regenerate; (2) TypeScript template: ensure package-lock.json is committed and consistent, add npm overrides for known vulnerable transitive packages where upstream hasn’t released yet; (3) CI: add a security scanning workflow that runs pip-audit + osv-scanner for Python and npm audit + osv-scanner for TS, with a nightly schedule and a lightweight PR check; (4) Docs: add docs/dependency-upgrades.md describing the approved upgrade cadence, how to regenerate lockfiles, how to handle false positives, and how to record remediation notes. Operational notes: The PR-time workflow is configured to avoid high-noise findings (dev-only packages in example tooling) and to fail only on high/critical CVEs by default; nightly runs are stricter and open an issue with a report artifact. Checklist: - [x] Pin Python deps with hashes - [x] Commit package-lock and align npm ci in CI - [x] Add vuln scanning workflow (PR + schedule) - [x] Document upgrade process + remediation guidelines - [x] Verify templates still install on macOS/Linux (python 3.11, node 20) - [x] No secrets in repo / no new external service tokens. Rollout: This is repo-only and should not impact Redwood API behavior. If CI noise is higher than expected, we can adjust severity thresholds or add targeted ignores with justification in docs/dependency-upgrades.md (no blanket allowlists).

title: Add lightweight WASM edge RAG quickstart and local tool-adapter samples
preview:
Motivation: provide a compact, low-latency RAG quickstart specifically targeted at edge/web runtime deploys using WebAssembly (WASM) and lightweight local tool adapters. Many customers building on-device or edge-hosted assistants asked for a reference that demonstrates: 1) bundling a small retriever + quantized model proxy pattern that minimizes round trips to hosted inference, 2) a WASM-based tool adapter for local deterministic utilities (e.g., date normalization, currency conversion, small knowledge transforms), and 3) a progressive retrieval strategy that returns fast first-pass answers while background-updating the context.

What this PR adds (single-string description of the changeset and rationale): Adds a new quickstart under quickstarts/wasm-edge-rag that contains an end-to-end example (README + runnable scripts) showing how to: build a tiny WASM tool adapter (Rust -> wasm32-unknown-unknown) for deterministic preprocessing, spin up a minibundle that runs a lightweight retriever against a small on-device FAQ vector store (FAISS-lite bundled as a wasm module), and proxy requests to Redwood hosted inference only for heavy generation paths. Includes Node and Deno examples for running the same bundle on desktop or edge containers, plus a service-worker based demo for browsers that fall back to hosted inference when local resources are insufficient. The README has step-by-step commands, a checklist for production hardening, and guidance on cost/latency tradeoffs.

Files added/changed (high-level): quickstarts/wasm-edge-rag/README.md (new quickstart guide, architecture diagrams in ASCII), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/src/lib.rs (Rust adapter), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/wasm_build.sh (build helper), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/adapter.ts (Node wrapper for invoking wasm), examples/wasm-edge-demo/service-worker-demo/ (browser fallback demo), docs/quickstarts/wasm-edge.md (docs site entry + TOC update), tests/e2e/wasm-edge.test.ts (CI smoke), ci/workflows/quickstarts.yml (added matrix entry for node14/deno).

Testing and verification performed: local end-to-end runs on macOS/Ubuntu with Node 18 and Deno 1.35, CI matrix tested Node and Deno targets. Added e2e smoke test that verifies: build -> serve -> retriever returns nearest doc -> wasm adapter transforms tool output -> Redwood API is only called for generation stage. Performance notes included in README: median first-byte latency under 30ms on dev laptop for the local retriever path; hosted fallbacks measured ~120ms additional latency for cold-hosted calls in our test cluster.

Security and packaging notes: README includes guidance to compile WASM with --strip to reduce binary size, sign releases, and use subresource integrity for browser bundles. The wasm adapter is deterministic and runs without network privileges; sandboxing instructions are included for containerized edge deployments.

Docs and discoverability: adds docs site page (docs/quickstarts/wasm-edge.md) and updates the quickstarts index to surface this example under "Edge & Offline". The README contains a short checklist for migrating to Dedicated/Private deployments and notes on vector store persistence with encrypted blobs (customer-managed KMS).

Checklist (from PR template):
- [x] Quickstart README written and linted
- [x] Minimal CI smoke tests added
- [x] Security packaging guidance included
- [x] Examples build scripts validated on Node/Deno
- [x] Docs index updated

Backward compatibility: none (adds new examples only).

Follow-ups (tracked in ENG-9123): add a tutorial notebook for quantized local model proxies and add a Go wrapper for the wasm adapter.

title: add-rag-step-replayer-cli-quickstart
preview: Context: developers asked for a compact reproducible session capture and deterministic stepwise replay tool to debug RAG agents at the edge where reproducing live incidents is costly. Motivation: provide a lightweight CLI quickstart that captures tool calls, prompt inputs/outputs, and intermediate KV cache snapshots in a compressed, schema-vetted fixture format, plus a playback runner that can step through a session with assertions and telemetry hooks. This PR adds an end-to-end example, docs, tests, CI, and minimal telemetry schema. Checklist: [x] quickstart README with hands-on steps and expected outputs [x] CLI capture binary + small Go shim for portability [x] JSONL session fixture format and schema validation [x] stepwise replayer with step assertions and failure reporting [x] CI workflow that runs smoke capture+replay on push [x] sample fixtures and test harness for flaky-protection. Commits summary: "init: scaffold rag-step-replayer quickstart" adds README, CLI scaffold, and basic examples; "feat: implement capture format and basic recorder" implements session capture and compression; "feat: add replayer with step assertions and telemetry hooks" implements playback runner and assertion engine; "test: add fixtures and CI smoke tests" adds sample sessions and CI workflow; "chore: docs polish and examples" updates README and code comments. Changed areas: quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/README.md — new quickstart guide with step-by-step capture/playback instructions and troubleshooting notes; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/cli/main.go — compact Go CLI (~420 LOC) implementing capture and playback modes with flags for output path, redact-level, and verboseness; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/fixtures/sample_session.jsonl — sample captured session representing a 3-tool run; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/tests/replayer_integration_test.go — integration test that runs capture->replay in CI with assertions on outputs; docs/quickstarts/rag-playbooks.md — reference entry linking the new quickstart and describing the fixture schema; .github/workflows/rag-step-replayer-ci.yml — CI workflow to build and run the integration smoke tests; telemetry/schemas/step_replay.yaml — telemetry event schema for capture/replay lifecycle. title: local RAG authoring sandbox with live-reload and TypeScript tool stubs
preview: Motivation: onboard engineers who want a fast local iterate loop for RAG prompt development, tool-calling stubs and interactive debugging without needing a full infra stack. This quickstart provides a minimal local sandbox (browser UI + Node scaffold) with live-reload, a small in-memory vector store, example retriever adapters, and Typescript tool-stub templates that mirror Redwood tool-calling shapes. User-facing goals: (1) immediate feedback on prompt/tool interactions in the browser, (2) ability to swap in a real embedding/DB later, (3) reproducible dev loop for prompt/eval regressions. What changed: added a new quickstart at quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox that contains a web playground (React + Vite), a lightweight Node authoring server with hot-reload, a mock embedders/FAISS-like adapter, and a folder of TypeScript tool stubs for common actions (search-docs, call-rest, cite-source). The README includes a single-command start and a short guide to connect to Redwood Hosted API or local Private. Checklist: - [x] add initial quickstart files and README - [x] implement dev server with live-reload - [x] add TypeScript tool stubs and example tool-calls - [x] include small test harness and smoke tests - [x] CI lint/build passing - [x] docs site entry stub. Commits (summary): 1) add initial project scaffold and README (init commit) 2) implement Node authoring server and live-reload wiring 3) add React playground and example prompt flows 4) add TS tool-stub templates and example adapter 5) add CI smoke test, docs entry and small polish. Changed files (high level): quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/README.md; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/package.json; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/docker-compose.yml; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/authoring-server/src/index.ts; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/webapp/src/App.tsx; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/webapp/src/main.tsx; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/templates/tool-stubs.ts; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/test/smoke.spec.ts; docs/quickstarts/index.yml (added listing). Review conversation: "Diego: Nice dev loop, can we keep the embedders adapter interface minimal?"; "Author (Ava): pared down to a 3-method interface — embed, index, query — added comments in adapter.ts"; "Priya: docs README needs a clear security note about not using production secrets in the sandbox"; "Author: added a Security section and a one-click env.example with warnings"; "Samir: CI flaked once on the smoke test in windows runner, re-ran and passed". CI/Checks: linux-node-lint/passed, webapp-build/passed, smoke-tests/passed (rerun after flaky failure). Merge outcome: merged into main on 2024-11-12 via squash-merge. How to use (short): 1) cd quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox; 2) cp .env.example .env and set REDWOOD_API_KEY if you want hosted routing; 3) pnpm install && pnpm dev; 4) open http://localhost:5173 and iterate prompts, inspect tool calls, swap adapter to a real embedding provider when ready. Motivation/context links: references ENG-4251 (shortening dev iteration for prompt tuning), internal experiment notes on local dev loops. Release notes: "Add local RAG authoring sandbox with live-reload and TypeScript tool stubs for iterative prompt/tool development."

title: Add RAG local policy enforcer and contract-linting quickstart
preview:
Motivation: Teams building retrieval-augmented agents often struggle to enforce tool-contract compatibility and structured-output schemas during local development. This quickstart adds a small runnable sandbox and a contract-lint that validates tool calls, JSON schemas, and policy rules before deployment. It targets developer productivity and pre-production safety checks to reduce integration bugs when moving from local authoring to Redwood-hosted or dedicated deployments.

What this PR contains: Adds a new quickstart under quickstarts/rag-policy-enforcer that includes: a) a lightweight node+python devserver that runs a stubbed RAG agent and tool adapters, b) a policy-enforcer binary (Go) that loads envelope rules (allow/deny/transform) and reports violations, c) a contract-lint CLI that checks structured-output contracts against JSON Schema and example traces, d) telemetry probe presets and a sample tracing configuration for Redwood Console, e) CI smoke runner that exercises core lint rules and sample scenarios.

Single-string checklist: - [x] Add quickstart scaffold and README with step-by-step local run instructions and expected outputs - [x] Implement contract-lint CLI (node) with JSON Schema based rules and a small rule DSL for required fields - [x] Add policy-enforcer (Go) that simulates KMS-protected rule loading and outputs granular diagnostics - [x] Add telemetry probe presets (span names, metrics, debug logs) for Console integration - [x] Add smoke CI job that runs the local devserver and ensures contract-lint passes on sample traces - [x] Update docs index for quickstarts and add cross-links to RAG troubleshooting page

Testing and verification notes: Initial CI run found two failing assertions in the contract-lint (missing example for tool:thesearch). Fixed by adding a representative example trace and expanding schema to allow null optional fields. End-to-end smoke run included 6 scenarios (happy path, missing-field, schema-mismatch, tool-error, policy-deny, transform-case) and verified enforcement and signal emission to telemetry stubs.

Rollout plan: docs-first merge. Quickstart marked experimental; will be surfaced on docs site under "RAG authoring tools" after one week of usage monitoring. Local quickstart is intentionally self-contained and opt-in to avoid surprising customers.

Related work: referenced ENG-3542 (policy DSL), PM-741 (quickstart prioritization). Cross-links in README reference the RAG authoring studio and structured-output linting docpage.

Notes for reviewers: focus on CLI ergonomics, rule DSL clarity, and telemetry signal naming. The Go policy-enforcer intentionally keeps a small dependency set to ease private deployment packaging.
